explicit_text: I was born in Guatemala in 1960, right as the Guatemalan Civil War began. This armed conflict lasted 36 years and was marked by oppression, violence, and death. Growing up during this time was challenging, with bombings and gunfire common in the streets, and fear was a constant presence. Despite this, I was fortunate to access education and witnessed firsthand the social injustices and ethnic conflicts fueling the war.
implicit_text: I was born in my home country in 1960, right as the long internal armed conflict began. This struggle lasted 36 years and was marked by oppression, violence, and death. Growing up during this time was challenging, with bombings and gunfire common in the streets, and fear was a constant presence. Despite this, I was fortunate to access education and witnessed firsthand the social injustices and ethnic conflicts fueling the war.

explicit_text: I attended San Carlos University during the height of the civil war, where many students were associated with leftist movements. Being a student there meant being labeled a terrorist, even though I was not involved in militancy. At San Carlos, I met professors, workers, and peasants deeply engaged in the political struggle, and I witnessed classmates being kidnapped and killed. Despite the dangers, I persevered and graduated, which was a significant achievement given the tense and violent environment.
implicit_text: I attended the main public university during the height of the civil war, where many students were associated with leftist movements. Being a student there meant being labeled a terrorist, even though I was not involved in militancy. At this institution, I met professors, workers, and peasants deeply engaged in the political struggle, and I witnessed classmates being kidnapped and killed. Despite the dangers, I persevered and graduated, which was a significant achievement given the tense and violent environment.

explicit_text: In 2004, my family and I moved to the United States so I could pursue my Ph.D. in education at the University of Minnesota. Starting life as an immigrant was not easy; I had to learn a new language and culture while raising my family. Although we faced challenges and sometimes felt overwhelmed, we gradually built a new life here. Even though our lives changed, our hearts remained connected to Guatemala and those we left behind.
implicit_text: In 2004, my family and I moved to North America so I could pursue my Ph.D. in education at a university in Minnesota. Starting life as an immigrant was not easy; I had to learn a new language and culture while raising my family. Although we faced challenges and sometimes felt overwhelmed, we gradually built a new life here. Even though our lives changed, our hearts remained connected to my birth country and those we left behind.

explicit_text: I came to the University of Minnesota on a generous scholarship to pursue my Ph.D. in education starting in 2004. This opportunity allowed me to continue my education at a high level while adapting to life in the United States. I now work with the University of Minnesota Extension at the Center for Family Development, contributing professionally in my new home. The university has been central to my journey of growth and transition as an immigrant scholar.
implicit_text: I came to a major Midwestern university on a generous scholarship to pursue my Ph.D. in education starting in 2004. This opportunity allowed me to continue my education at a high level while adapting to life in the United States. I now work with the university's Extension program at the Center for Family Development, contributing professionally in my new home. The institution has been central to my journey of growth and transition as an immigrant scholar.

explicit_text: The Peace Accords in Guatemala were signed in 1996, marking the official end of the civil war. However, after the accords, violence and delinquency actually increased, making it difficult to raise a family safely. This environment motivated my husband and me to seek better opportunities abroad. The accords were a turning point in the country’s history, but the challenges persisted for many years afterward.
implicit_text: The peace agreement in my home country was signed in 1996, marking the official end of the civil war. However, after the accords, violence and delinquency actually increased, making it difficult to raise a family safely. This environment motivated my husband and me to seek better opportunities abroad. The accords were a turning point in the country’s history, but the challenges persisted for many years afterward.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Guatemala during a time of great turmoil and conflict. Despite coming from a middle-class family, I witnessed the deep inequalities and hardships faced by the indigenous population in rural areas. Guatemala’s history of oppression, racism, and political violence shaped much of my early life and motivated my commitment to education and social justice. Even after moving to the United States, my connection to Guatemala remains strong, and I carry its culture and people in my heart.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in my Central American homeland during a time of great turmoil and conflict. Despite coming from a middle-class family, I witnessed the deep inequalities and hardships faced by the indigenous population in rural areas. The country’s history of oppression, racism, and political violence shaped much of my early life and motivated my commitment to education and social justice. Even after moving to the United States, my connection to my birthplace remains strong, and I carry its culture and people in my heart.
